zHTest that the semantics relating to the 'fromlist' argument are correct.
util
ReturnValuea-
The use of fromlist influences what import returns.
    If direct ``import ...`` statement is used, the root module or package is
    returned [import return]. But if fromlist is set, then the specified module
    is actually returned (whether it is a relative import or not)
    [from return].
    c
pkg.__init__
pkg.module
	meta_path
pkg)
Z	mock_spec
import_state
__import__
assertEqual
__name__
self
importer
module
A/usr/lib64/python3.9/test/test_importlib/import_/test_fromlist.py
test_return_from_import
z#ReturnValue.test_return_from_importc
attr
fromlist
mock_modulesr
test_return_from_from_import
z(ReturnValue.test_return_from_from_importN)
__module__
__qualname__
__doc__r
HandlingFromlista
Using fromlist triggers different actions based on what is being asked
    of it.
    If fromlist specifies an object on a module, nothing special happens
    [object case]. This is even true if the object does not exist [bad object].
    If a package is being imported, then what is listed in fromlist may be
    treated as a module to be imported [module]. And this extends to what is
    contained in __all__ when '*' is imported [using *]. And '*' does not need
    to be the only name in the fromlist [using * with others].
    c
test_object5
HandlingFromlist.test_objectc
non_existentr
assertFalse
hasattrr
test_nonexistent_object<
z(HandlingFromlist.test_nonexistent_objectc

z.HandlingFromlist.test_nonexistent_from_packagec
i_do_not_existr(

z[HandlingFromlist.test_module_from_package_triggers_ModuleNotFoundError.<locals>.module_coder
pkg.mod)
